sentences.text: Callwood.
Yes, Santa.
Definitely.
My mother, as you know, as I previously said, she was taken to the hospital very sick.
She had two operations during that time.
She was in a Belgium hospital.
I wanted to make that clear.
While she was there, there were about 32 Jewish people in that hospital.
A disgruntled person denounced the Jews who were in that hospital.
And unfortunately, my mother was taken away from the hospital bed and placed in the last transport to Auschwitz Birkenau, which was number 28.
When she came there, she told me the stories.
And I also learned from other people.
Because it was the last transport, primarily the Jewish women there were of Hungarian descent.
And when you were married or coming with a child, you did not come to be a laborer.
You came to be exterminated.
But if you were single, that's when they would keep you into the camp for labor purposes, until they figured that you couldn't handle it no more because your body couldn't sustain it.
And then they would give you the shower of gas.
My mother was very sweet and tender.
And the Hungarian women knew that she had three children in hiding.
So they said to her, we must do whatever we can to keep you surviving.
Because those children, as long as they are in hiding, and they have not been exterminated, we must make sure that you stay alive so that you can be with them after the war.
One day, when they were standing in line to have their ration of food, which was skinned potatoes in water, they said to her, why don't you go for a second one?
You need the strength.
So she went and asked for a second portion.
And an SS woman gave her the loudest, meanest, hardest smack on her head, and she went right against the wall.
And she got so badly hurt that they had to take her in the infirmary.
The other thing was that the lice were everywhere.
And as you know, they were sleeping on boards, right-- one-- next to each other.
And my mother, knowing that her children were not dead, that they were in hiding, had a purpose to want to survive.
And when these Hungarian women-- I'm coming back to this one here-- heard what had happened, they let out a rumor that the Jewish woman of the camp are going to lie in wait for the SS woman.
It was a defiance brought by the, by the brutality, that one woman of another nation showed to another woman who was her prisoner.
And the Hungarian women did not like that.
There were very strong and stoic women.
The-- they were mostly singles.
Those who stayed on were singles.
The pretty ones were used as field whores.
So the female, the Jewish female, if she was very pretty and she was not married with children, her lot was being to serve the Nazis in the camp.
My mother went to work in a tank factory there.
A tank, a little tiny woman, working on the tank with the chains.
She had to work with chains.
They weighted a ton.
But somehow-- the human strength, where it comes from, I don't know-- she survived that.
But coming back to the other issue about this stifling situation in the barracks.
You couldn't wash yourself.
Lice was everywhere, et cetera.
How did my mother get her inspiration to keep going day after day after day in this miserable condition?
She would go out in front of the door, when that was possible, when the sun was shining, whenever that was possible.
That was not always possible.
And she would just let the sun rays come to her to give her the feeling of warmth and of light.
And you know, when you give yourself gold lights to your face, to your eyes, it perks up the spirit.
And she was a lady who never wanted to fight with anybody or argue with anybody.
So that one day she was carrying dental equipment, and a SS woman [? caught ?] pity on her because it was so much equipment to carry.
And this SS woman volunteered to help her with the equipment, right there in that camp.
So amongst all these humans, good and bad existed, side by side, no matter what uniform you wore.
And my mother somehow knew to stay away from that kind of trouble where she would not be picked out to the gas.
She almost did, however.
She was standing in line.
And Dr.
Mengele was sitting with his bloodhounds, and the piano ticker, and his monocle.
And each day he would look at all the prisoners.
And my mother would bend down to shoe her laces.
And her spine came out this high.
He walked over to her, and he lifted her by force, turned her around.
And her skin was flushed with the most beautiful rose pink, red color.
Well, she looked too healthy to be sent to the shower of gas.
So he put her back to right.
Right, you work.
Left, you go to the showers.
Those were the daily experiences that my mother went through in Auschwitz Birkenau.
She was there-- since it was the last transport-- she was there, I believe, almost a year.
And my father, who was taken in the latter part of '42. he was taken to a labor camp in France.
Now, here's a classic story.
We, the Wagschal's, we love food.
We love good food.
My father was very disillusioned in that labor camp because he didn't like the food.
And he decided, when he talked to the other inmates-- or teammates.
How, how should we call other prisoners of war-- regarding his desire to go to Paris.
He wants to leave the labor camp.
And they said, [NON-ENGLISH], you want to go to Paris?
All you have to do is walk out the labor camp.
Well, literally, my father, on a day of work in the bush, in the, in the field, in daylight, walked away.
Ran as fast as he could, hid in barns, and arrived in two separate villages.
Kept going into barns, and changed clothes, found something to change himself, maybe in farm clothes, what have you.
Couldn't speak a word of French.
All we could speak is Polish, Dutch-- which is Flemish in Belgium-- and Yiddish.
On the third day, he arrived in a village.
And he again hid in a barn.
This time, he was caught by the person who belonged-- who the barn belonged to.
It turns out to be-- and this men recognized that my father was a Jew, even though he didn't have that Jewish face.
And he recognized immediately that he was a runaway.
And who he turns out to be, he was the head of the marquee in that region, which is the underground of the French underground, and also the mayor of that village.
This man gave him sanctuary, gave him a name to carry, as long as he stayed in his house.
Because my father said, I want to go to Paris.
They couldn't speak Eng-- neither one could speak each other's language.
But yet, it communicated out that my father wanted to go to Paris.
And the mayor says, you cannot go to Paris.
You will be shot.
By the time you come there, you'll be shot.
And so he gave him that sanctuary.
And my father's name during the stay he was there, between '43 and '45, his name was known as Jean Pierre.
He had to play the role of a deaf, dumb.
And while he worked in the fields, and in the barn, and slept in the barn of this mayor and chief marquee of this region, one day while he was in the barn, Rommel came for a visit.
And Rommel stood right in front of my father and did not know that he was an escaped Jew from a labor camp.
And my father survived this because of the generosity of this beautiful human being.
Again, a miracle.
And when my father returned after the war to Antwerp, these two men each year corresponded with each other until the immigration to the United States.
I have absolutely no idea.
And if I had paid more attention as a child when my father was getting the correspondence from this person, who was, at that time, the mayor, and I don't know how long he was mayor.
And if I had taken the notes down-- I regret this.
If I regret anything, it's not having been sensitive enough in my teenage years to take down notes on paper about certain names and cert-- I remember things that my mother and father talked about.
But certain names and things like that should be written down on paper.
And I never-- until today, and I asked my brothers-- the name of that labor camp.
I do not know the name of the labor camp.
And of course, it was a labor camp where you went to work for the Germans.
But if you were weak and so forth, they, they knew to exterminate you.
Not precisely there, but they knew to take you away for that purpose.
In one word, Jews were used for two purposes.
One, to elimination, because to them, you had no value.
And the other one, to keep you working until they knew what to do with you, because they needed certain work loads done.
Also, the experimentations, which everybody has documented already, regarding the use of human skin.
The experimentation of women's fertilities, all these things that were used from the human body to experiment in the name of experimentation, that truly to torture.
That was really behind it all.
And when people keep saying it never happened-- is beyond my comprehension, why people cannot accept the truth.
Do they need far more to go than to see that in 1994 a half a million people were slaughtered in a few weeks time in Rwanda, Africa.
There has been exterminations on this Earth when there is a genocide.
Genocides happen on and off on this Earth amongst us humans.
I've been trying to find the answers.
Why is genocide happening amongst human beings?
And it is not just because of religion, or because of money.
Now it is only because of power and greed.
The greed to be in control, the greed to take the empowerment of someone else, bestow it on yourself, and have the total control of what you feel belongs to you and nobody else.
This is the same way here with the Nazi war.
When I sat in a train in 1969 going from Muenchen to Hamburg, which is a good, nine-hour train ride, I sat with a German lady in a compartment talking for all these hours.

And she told me, a single woman, that when she goes outside of Germany and a young man comes over to her to want to date her, and they find out she's from German ancestry and from Germany, they don't want to date her.
And she asked me, why don't they want to date me?
I had no part in that war.
And I told her the reason is because normally, wars is between soldiers on foot or in the air.
But never, when you take women, children, old people, and exterminate them.
Then it's not war.
Its genocide.
And genocide nobody forgets, and they can never forgive.

I says, what Hitler did in the name of the Reich to implement that the Jews were making all the money, et cetera, and that the Germans were kept-- I says, you all were kept in a very, very weak position, because you all believed him.
So what is the bottom line?
Germany will never be the same again because Germany will always suffer the persecution of one man, who gave orders to many others.
This is your history.
And that is why people never forget, because that history is all over the world.
And you have to thank your leader for having done this to you and the rest of your people.
You have nothing to do with it, but he made you a victim when he created such a genocide.
Yes.
She was liberated by the Russians.
They came to the camp.
And the Russians, in her case, were her liberators.
Also, when we all came together in Antwerp, there used to be-- and there still is, I showed it to Tony-- a park known as [NON-ENGLISH].
It was full of mines, full of booby traps.

So what they did immediately after the war, the GIs, the Americans GIs, used the German soldiers-- and the park was close to the public-- to clear away every booby trap and every mine so that the park could be reopened to the public.
And you know, children are teasers.
And as children, we would go out there.
And we would tease the-- I remember, I teased them too-- the Germans who were at labor.
And one time, one German-- this is after the war now-- lifted his shovel to hit a child.
And a GI point blank killed him.
And that's when the commander said, no more.
We cannot allow children around here.
And this has to be done without this kind of attendance.
Because you know, children have a tendency to be curious, and go, and so forth.
But I went in that park many, many, many times.
And I loved being in that park.
So the thing is, no food in concentration camp.
You did not eat.
That's why when a human being goes down from 135 pounds to 68 pounds, isn't this not enough witness as to what happened, just internally to a human being, in the concentration camp?
This was not a diet.
This was forced hunger.
Did your mom and dad talk to you about the experience that they had when they found you?
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes, especially my mom.
She detailed a lot of-- she had her number.
I never took it down.
I should have taken down her number.
She was buried with it in 1979.
She died in Brooklyn, New York.
And up to her death, she was the most sweetest, most loving, had not a malice in her heart.
She was just wonderful.
And my father, he died in 1982.
And all the way through, we would sit at the table, especially in Belgium.
Because at that time, I was very religious.
And I would sing [INAUDIBLE] with him.
And our eyes would tear while we're singing [INAUDIBLE].
This is religious chanting on Friday night, Sabbath meal.
And I was the caretaker.
I took care of my mom, of my two brothers, my father.
And then my sister was born, and I took her.
I was a mama for 11 and 1/2 years.
And she's wonderful.
She's a mother of two children and is a media librarian, and very good one.
So we survive.
We come and we survive.
But the experience in Belgium after the war, the anti-Semitism was enormous.
Because I was always called [NON-ENGLISH], dirty Jewess.
So my whole life was spent with-- I'm a dirty Jew.
But if I'm so dirty, how can I pray?
And all these questions start coming in.
And there was one particular boy whom I had an eye on.
And whenever the carousels and the-- would come once a year, I would hang out there.
And he would call me, [NON-ENGLISH].
And then one day I says, no, no, no, no.
Sabina, do something about this.
So I went over to him.
And I says, listen.
You've called me dirty Jew so many times.
But do you realize when you call me a dirty Jew, you're belittling your God, Jesus Christ?
He is my brother.
He's my fellow Jew.
And when you say dirty Jew, you're saying Jesus was dirty.
I think I was 10 years old when I told him this.
And you know what?
We became the best of friends.
There is a way of overcoming, if you take the time to communicate.
And then the other experience we had, Tony and I, my husband who is also an entertainer, we went to from Kiel, Germany to Norway on the Prince [?
Valiant ?] boat to do a show on crossing.
This was 1969.
On the way back from Oslo, Norway to Kiel, Germany, he had to do another show.
And he had an album.
By the way, it got four stars in under Folk Album.
And a little plaque never hurts, does it?
[CHUCKLING]
And this man, this couple, asked to purchase one, and would we sit with them?
So we sat down with them.
And there was another young man with them.
I could only speak with them in German.
And so I asked the questions about Germany and how things are going, et cetera, et cetera.
And then I asked-- as you know, I'm inter-racially married with a gentleman from the Virgin Islands.
I said, do you have any problems in Germany for black people?
And he says, oh, no.
No, no, no, no.
Black people can live anyplace, go to school, no problem.
But we have another problem.
I said, what kind of problem do you have?
He says, we have a Jewish problem.
Oh, you have a Jewish problem?
What kind of Jewish problem do you have?
They make all the money.
They are controllers.
And I said-- I never let him know I was Jewish-- tell me.
Where does the Jew make the money-- how do you say?
Where does the non-Jew have the money that the Jew makes from?
Because he has to make it from the non-Jew as well.
So where does the non-Jew have the money to make, where the Jew makes from the non-Jew?
I said, why do you feel that the Jewish people are a problem?
Because they use their brains for survival?
I says, if you want to be rich, you can be rich.
Nobody stops you, nothing to do with religions, only what you think you want to decide what you want to do with your, your life.
And then I started to philosophize on Hitler.

And that's when I gave that ceremony about the legacy that Hitler left behind is bottom line a constant reminder by the world that Germany has created and has finished off a genocide of a group of people, as well as gypsies, and all kinds of other people, that they felt didn't belong in their society.

And I said, your children, and your children, and your children, everybody is going to suffer the implication of that genocide because that man kept you weak by implicating the Jew as the wrong person.
And I says, when you take one side to use as a weapon and keep you down in a weak spot so that he can fulfill his dreams, he victimized both sides.
One, he exterminated.
And the other one he has kept in perpetual-- what-- how should I say that-- a forever stigma of what your leader has done in the world with his war.
Do you know that man, who was so pro Nazi and so, [? actung ?] like, became meek after three and a half hours of discussion.
And then the following day in Kiel Germany, when we arrived, when we were descending the walkway from the boat, the young man called out my name and asked to speak with me before we leave.
He says, I want you to know, I could not sleep the whole night because of what you said.
Thank you for enlightening.
I will transfer the message to other Germans.
The reason I bring this up is, we who suffered, and we who have other descendants beyond us, who learned first-hand from us, we must never, never sit in the seat of God in judgment.
What we must do is learn to communicate, understand why the human mind does something like this and so it shall never, ever happen again.
But it does happen again.
Prove again.
There is another country in the world-- I'm trying to remember the name of that country right now.
I'm a little blanked out-- where a-- at least over a million people were exterminated, someplace in the orient.
I can't remember right now the name.
And they backed their calendar to zero.
And that was, I believe, in the '70s.
Cambodia.
Thank you.
So you see, history repeats itself.
If not by the Germans, then by the Cambodian.
Not the Cambodian, then the Rwandans.
So we must explore truly why we, the human race, are continuing on the same vein that happened in Germany.
